# Verify `sesam user remove-recipient --all-except` keeps only the recipients
# named by --recipient and drops every other one in a single call, rotating the
# audit key so the removed keys lose access while the kept key survives.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Give bob three recipients: his own key, carol's key (a real second device we
# can test lock-out with) and an extra device key with no private half.
exec sesam tell --user bob --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY --recipient $CAROL_PUBKEY --recipient age1gtfhwme5a3sg9r7sp9vqu5awdzft4xd5qjesdq8lhry5wy8tfsfqw7fgt6 --group dev

# All three of bob's recipients decrypt the log to start with.
exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
exec sesam --identity $CAROL_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl

# Keep only bob's own key; carol and the extra device are removed at once.
exec sesam user remove-recipient --user bob --all-except --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY

# The audit log records the removal of both dropped keys.
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"user.rm_recipients"'
stdout '"bob"'

# The kept key still decrypts, the removed key is locked out (key rotated).
exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
! exec sesam --identity $CAROL_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl

exec sesam verify

# The short alias behaves the same and, with only the kept key left, is a no-op
# that leaves bob's remaining recipient in place.
exec sesam user remove-recipient --user bob -a --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY
exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
